questions.question: A streaming algorithm processes N items arriving one at a time. What is the typical memory bound for a useful streaming algorithm?
options: O(N) — must store all items
O(N log N) — one item per comparison
O(polylog N) — sub-linear in stream length
O(1) — constant, independent of N and any accuracy parameter
questions.explanation: Streaming algorithms trade exactness for space, requiring O(polylog N) memory — typically O(1/ε · log(1/δ)) or O(log N). O(N) defeats the purpose (you'd store the whole stream), O(1) is too restrictive for useful accuracy, and O(N log N) is even larger.

questions.question: In reservoir sampling with k slots, after processing the i-th element (i > k), what is the probability that any specific earlier element remains in the reservoir?
options: k/i
(i−k)/i
questions.explanation: By induction: the first k items each have probability k/k = 1 at step k. At step i > k, each existing item is replaced with probability (k/i) × (1/k) = 1/i, so it survives with probability (1 − 1/i). Multiplying from step k+1 to i gives k/i. Every element in the stream has the same k/i probability — the sample is exactly uniform.

questions.question: A Count-Min Sketch uses w columns and d rows. After N insertions, what does the ε-δ guarantee state about the estimate for element x?
options: The estimate equals the true count with probability 1 − δ
The estimate is within ±εN of the true count with probability 1 − δ
The estimate is always ≥ true count, and exceeds it by at most εN with probability ≥ 1 − δ
The estimate has relative error at most ε with probability 1 − δ
questions.explanation: The Count-Min Sketch always overestimates (never underestimates) because min-of-inflated-counts ≥ true count. The guarantee is: true_count ≤ estimate ≤ true_count + εN with probability ≥ 1 − δ. The error is additive (εN), not multiplicative, and is one-sided.

questions.question: HyperLogLog estimates cardinality by hashing elements and tracking the maximum number of leading zeros per register. Why does it use the harmonic mean across registers instead of the arithmetic mean?
options: The harmonic mean is faster to compute incrementally
The harmonic mean reduces the impact of outlier registers that overestimate
The harmonic mean guarantees the estimate is always an integer
The arithmetic mean would produce a biased low estimate
questions.explanation: Individual registers can have inflated max-leading-zero counts due to chance. The arithmetic mean is sensitive to these outliers. The harmonic mean (m / Σ 2^(−register[j])) effectively down-weights extreme values, producing a more robust cardinality estimate. This is why HyperLogLog outperforms LogLog (which uses the arithmetic mean).

questions.question: Why can't a streaming algorithm simply store all elements to count distinct values?
options: Because hash tables are too slow
Because the stream may be too large to fit in memory, requiring sublinear space
Because elements arrive out of order
Because streaming algorithms can only handle integers
questions.explanation: Streaming algorithms process data in one pass with limited memory (often O(log n) space). Storing all elements would require O(n) space. HyperLogLog estimates distinct count using hash-based probabilistic counting in just ~1.5 KB regardless of stream size.
